stem: In a medical treatment study comparing Drug X vs. Drug Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher recovery rate (73.3% vs 62.1%). However, when stratified by disease severity: In mild cases (n_X=20, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 83% vs Treatment Y's 78%. In severe cases (n_X=120, n_Y=20): Treatment X achieves 59% vs Treatment Y's 52%. The true population proportion of mild cases is uncertain, estimated between 38% and 52%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to severe cases (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.06, 0.062], point estimate: 0.061. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.1 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=62.1%, Y=73.3% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In mild cases: X=83% > Y=78%. In severe cases: X=59% > Y=52%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Drug X is applied more to severe cases (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
Not considering that additional unobserved confounders could further modify the comparison
